docs.title: Logging In
docs.description: Any user with a valid email and password can log into Shakti and will automatically be directed to the control panel.

docs.title: Resetting Password
docs.description: To reset a password, follow these steps:

docs.title: Signing Up
docs.description: Yntraa offers a a simple and user-friendly platform for creating a subscriber cloud account, enabling access to its full range of cloud services and features. Whether you're registering as an individual or on behalf of an organization, the platform ensures a seamless sign-up experience.

docs.title: About One Yotta Account Centre
docs.description: The Account Centre on the Yntraa cloud console is a non-technical (commercial and support) dashboard used for viewing and managing all non-technical aspects of your Yntraa cloud account. You can access the Account Centre by clicking on any item in the user menu at the top-right, the help/support icon in the header, or the wallet icon in the header.

docs.title: Invoices and Credit Notes
docs.description: Invoices are generated at the end of each billing cycle for the services consumed during the billing cycle. Invoices can be accessed from the Billing > Invoices & Credit Notes section on the main navigation panel.

docs.title: Organisation/Account Profile
docs.description: Using the Profile section, you can manage your organisation account profile on Yntraa cloud console. The profile management section can be accessed from the Account Centre in the top helper bar by clicking on the organisation.. Account profiles include the following:

docs.title: Payments
docs.description: Yntraa cloud console supports three ways of making payments or adding money to the wallet:

docs.title: Standard Agreements
docs.description: Standard agreements and documents are available on the Yntraa cloud console Account Centre. Whenever any of these documents is amended or updated, you will be prompted to accept the modified documents on your next login.

docs.title: Subscriptions and Services
docs.description: A subscription is a record created whenever a new resource is purchased or a service is activated. In other words, a subscription is a financial ‘agreement’ that links the item that has been purchased, its value, and its renewal frequency.

docs.title: Support and Tickets
docs.description: Yntraa cloud console provides SLA-driven support to all subscriber customers in accordance with the terms and conditions outlined in the Service Level Agreement. To access the SLA, navigate to the User icon in the top helper bar and select Account under Account Centre.

docs.title: Team and Child User Management
docs.description: Team members or child users can be onboarded using the TEAM section from the Account Centre. Yntraa cloud console allows you to add billing, technical, or other admin users who can log in to your account and perform operations.

docs.title: Unbilled Charges
docs.description: All subscriptions incur prorated charges during an account’s billing cycle. These charges, also known as unbilled charges, can be accessed from Billing > Unbilled Charges in the main navigation panel.

docs.title: Wallet and Transactions
docs.description: For all practical purposes, the service balance acts as your wallet balance or purchase capacity at any given point of time. The service balance is an aggregation of your credit limit, total payments made, and the total charges incurred on your account. Your service balance is always displayed on the top bar and gets updated in real-time when a transaction or a charge is recorded.

docs.title: About Autoscale
docs.description: The Autoscale feature allows a system to adjust its computing resources based on the changing workload automatically. In virtual machines or instances, autoscaling helps ensure that an application or service has the right amount of resources to handle varying levels of demand efficiently. The key concept behind autoscaling is dynamic resource allocation. When demand for a particular application or service increases, autoscaling automatically adds more computing resources (such as instances or virtual machines) to handle the load. Conversely, during periods of lower demand, autoscaling reduces the number of resources to optimize cost and efficiency.

docs.title: Creating Autoscale Groups
docs.description: To create Autoscale Groups, follow these steps:

next.title: Using Static Password In Autoscale Group’s VMs

docs.title: Requirements for Autoscale
docs.description: - Yntraa now uses the native Autoscale feature of ACS. This feature is available with ACS version 4.18.0.0 onwards.

docs.title: Using Static Password In Autoscale Group’s VMs
docs.description: 1. Create a VPC and add a network tier inside the VPC.

docs.title: About Kubernetes
docs.description: Yntraa cloud console users the ability to deploy highly-available Kubernetes clusters for their cloud-native development, deployment and DevOps use cases. The Yntraa Kubernetes Service (AK8s) extends basic and advanced cluster management functionalities via the Cloud Console UI and the kubectl utility.

docs.title: About Kubernetes Dashboard
docs.description: The Kubernetes Dashboard is a web-based user interface that provides a visual representation of a cluster's resources and enables easier management and monitoring. Here's an overview of the dashboard:

docs.title: Accessing a Cluster using the Command Line
docs.description: Kubernetes clusters can be accessed and controlled from the command line using the kubeconfig manifest for the cluster. Each cluster has a unique manifest which is required to identify and target the cluster using the kubectl utility.

docs.title: Accessing the Kubernetes Dashboard
docs.description: To access the dashboard in Kubernetes version 1.24 and onwards, follow these steps:

docs.title: Cluster Operations
docs.description: Kubernetes clusters created on Yntraa cloud console allow for a set of management operations from the Cloud Console UI. While these options can be controlled using kubectl, these are provided on the UI for quick and easy access.

docs.title: Creating a Kubernetes Cluster
docs.description: To create a Kubernetes cluster, follow these steps:

docs.title: Ingress Networking on Kubernetes Clusters
docs.description: To view networking, navigate to Kubernetes and select a cluster details. This section manage a cluster's firewalls and ingress networking. Here, Port forwarding and load balancing rules can also be managed using kubectl.

docs.title: Overview
docs.description: To view the below details, navigate to Kubernetes, select a Kubernetes Cluster, and access the Overview tab.

next.title: Accessing a Cluster using the Command Line

docs.title: Scaling Kubernetes Clusters
docs.description: Yntraa cloud console allows for manual and automatic cluster scaling. Clusters scaling can be configured from the Nodes section of cluster details or from the Operations section of cluster details.

previous.title: Accessing a Cluster using the Command Line

docs.title: Viewing Kubernetes Cluster Details
docs.description: Yntraa cloud console offers a detailed view of Kubernetes clusters on the UI. Yntraa also brings the full power and accessibility of cluster management via the kubectl interface.

docs.title: About Linux Instances
docs.description: Yntraa cloud console offers a highly usable and visual way of working with and operating Linux Instances using the Cloud Console. You can access all the Linux Instances available in your account in the following way:

docs.description: You can connect to Linux Instances via SSH sessions using passwords (less secure) or SSH key pairs (most secure)

docs.title: About RHEL Instances
docs.description: Instances are computing units that provide you with resources to run your applications/workloads. Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) Instances are virtual machines that run the images of RHEL Operating System.

docs.description: Alerts get triggered whenever a configured condition is met. You can create multiple alerts on an instance. Alerts are sent to recipients that you can define and manage.

docs.description: You can connect to RHEL Instances via SSH sessions using passwords (less secure) or SSH key pairs (most secure)

docs.description: Instances are computing units that provide you with resources to run your applications/workloads. Windows Instances are virtual machines that run the images of Windows Operating Systems.

docs.title: About DNS Management
docs.description: Apiculus servers can be used as authoritative name server(s) for managing DNS records. If the Service is enabled, Cloud Console users can bring their own domains (BYOD) to use with the workloads created using Apiculus-powered clouds.

next.title: Linking Domains to Apiculus Cloud Console

docs.title: Linking Domains to Apiculus Cloud Console
docs.description: The Apiculus-powered cloud has nameservers if the service provider has enabled this service. These name servers are simply fully-qualified domain names (FQDN) for the servers that store all the domain/zone information. Typically, these name servers will be in the following format:

docs.title: Managing DNS Records
docs.description: DNS records and subdomains can be managed on Apiculus Cloud Console to be used with Instances, IPv4 Addresses and other workloads created on Apiculus-powered clouds. This only requires a domain to be linked with the Apiculus nameservers.

previous.title: Linking Domains to Apiculus Cloud Console

docs.title: Switching to Another DNS Provider
docs.description: In case you don't want to use the DNS Management Service on your Apiculus-powered cloud, you can always switch back to another provider of choice. To do so, head to your domain name provider's control panel and update the nameserver details to those of the DNS provider you wish to use.

docs.title: About IPv4-based Networking
docs.description: IPv4 Addresses form the backbone of networking operations on Apiculus. Internally, IPv4 Addresses are treated as a monetisable Service on Apiculus. However, the use of IPv4 Addresses is implicit as part of other essential Services.

next.title: Managing VPN Gateways and Site-to-Site VPN

docs.title: Establishing Site-to-Site VPN Between Two VPCs
docs.description: A Site-to-Site Virtual Private Network (VPN) provides a secure and encrypted connection between two Virtual Private Clouds (VPC), enabling seamless communication across geographically separated networks. This setup is commonly used to connect different environments (such as production and development) or extend on-premises infrastructure to the cloud.

previous.title: Using Remote Access VPN with VPC

docs.title: Managing VPN Gateways and Site-to-Site VPN
docs.description: Creating a VPN Gateway

next.title: Using Remote Access VPN with VPC

docs.title: Using Remote Access VPN with VPC
docs.description: To use a VPN client to connect to your VPC, follow these steps:

next.title: Establishing Site-to-Site VPN Between Two VPCs

docs.title: About L2 Networks
docs.description: L2 networks, often called data link layer networks, play a crucial role in computer networking by establishing direct connections between devices in a local network and ensuring data packets are delivered accurately to their intended destination.

docs.title: Adding New L2 Network
docs.description: To add a new L2 network, click the New Network button. The following screen appears:

docs.title: About Load Balancer Instances
docs.description: The Apiculus Load Balancer Instances (LBI) Service allows Cloud Console users to create load balancer appliances, which are Linux Instances pre-loaded with a Citrix NetScaler VPX image. LBI can be used with VPC networks to achieve various traffic management features that are not possible using the standard VPC-based load balancing.

docs.title: Activating the NetScaler VPX Control Panel
docs.description: The following steps can be used for activating the NetScaler VPX control panel and accessing it after activation:

docs.title: Creating a Load Balancer Instance
docs.description: Load Balancer Instances can be created inside a VPC by following these simple steps:

next.title: Activating the NetScaler VPX Control Panel

docs.title: About NAT Gateways
docs.description: A Network Address Translation (NAT) gateway is a networking component in cloud and on-premises environments that enables resources within a private subnet (such as virtual machines or containers) to connect to the internet or other external networks, without exposing these resources directly to incoming connections from those external networks. This helps in enhancing security in cloud infrastructure by keeping resources in private subnets isolated from direct public access.

docs.title: Instances
docs.description: This section lists all the instances that are a part of this Virtual Firewall.

docs.title: IPv4 Addresses
docs.description: Each virtual gateway comes with a default IPv4 address that belongs to the Virtual Gateway governing the network. The default (source - NAT) IPv4 address can be used to access the Virtual Gateway publicly or via Remote Access (RA) VPN.

docs.title: Operations
docs.description: You can perform the following operations in the Operations section:

next.title: Port Forwarding for VM via VNF

docs.title: Overview
docs.description: Navigate to the Overview tab to view the following details:

docs.title: Viewing and Managing NAT Gateways
docs.description: To manage a NAT gateway, follow these steps:

docs.title: Port Forwarding for VM via VNF
docs.description: This section outlines the steps to configure port forwarding for a Virtual Machine (VM) using a Virtual Network Function (VNF) in a cloud environment. Port forwarding allows external clients to securely access services (for example SSH, HTTP) hosted on the VM by mapping ports from the VNF’s public IP to the VM’s private IP. This approach improves network segmentation and control by routing all incoming traffic through the VNF layer.

docs.title: Port Forwarding with Public IP
docs.description: In Apiculus cloud platform, an additional Public IP enables you to expose internal services hosted on virtual machines (VMs) to the internet by creating dedicated port forwarding rules. This approach is especially useful when multiple external IPs are needed for services such as SSH, HTTP, or custom applications. By assigning specific public IPs to particular services or VMs, you can achieve greater control, flexibility, and network segmentation.

previous.title: Port Forwarding for VM via VNF

docs.title: Accessing the VFI Control Panel
docs.description: The Virtual Firewall Service is delivered as an integration using OS images pre-loaded with pfSense or FortiGate VM.

previous.title: Deploying VNF and VM Within VNF

docs.title: Adding Instances to a VFI-based Network
docs.description: Instances can be created and placed in the L2-LAN (Free Networks) created by the Virtual Firewall. These Instances use special Linux OS images, which are available as a separate OS Collection. Users can create Linux Instances, and choose the appropriate options/controls to create an Instance in the Virtual Firewall network.

next.title: Deploying VNF and VM Within VNF

docs.title: Creating a Virtual Firewall
docs.description: To create a Virtual Firewall, follow these steps:

next.title: Adding Instances to a VFI-based Network

docs.title: Deploying VNF and VM Within VNF
docs.description: This section provides a step-by-step guide for deploying a Virtual Network Function (VNF)—specifically a virtual firewall—and provisioning a Virtual Machine (VM) behind it using the Apiculus Cloud platform. This setup allows users to route VM traffic through the VNF, enabling secure and segmented network environments.

previous.title: Adding Instances to a VFI-based Network

docs.title: About Firewall Instances
docs.description: Virtual Firewalls enables you to use Virtual Firewalls in a Virtualized Network Function (VNF) style, which include support for multiple VLANs and Public IPv4 addresses and automated service activation. This allows for more robust firewall management capabilities.

docs.title: Configuring Alerts
docs.description: To view the configured alerts or configure new ones, navigate to the Virtual Firewall and access the Alerts tab.

docs.title: Networking Management
docs.description: To view the networks that of particular Instance, navigate to the Virtual Firewall and access the Networking tab.

docs.title: Operations
docs.description: To view all available Instance operations, navigate to the Networking, select a Virtual Firewall and access the Operations tab.

docs.title: Overview
docs.description: To view the below details, navigate to the Firewall Security, select the Virtual Firewall and access the Overview tab.

docs.title: Reconfiguring Virtual Firewall
docs.description: To view available reconfiguration options, navigate to the Networking section and select a Virtual Firewall and access the Reconfigure tab.

docs.title: Snapshots
docs.description: To view all the Snapshots taken for Instance, navigate to the Networking, select a Virtual Firewall and access the Snapshots tab.

docs.title: Viewing Graphs and Utilization
docs.description: To view the available graphs and monitor the instance in real-time, navigate to the Virtual Firewall and access the Graphs tab.

docs.title: Viewing Virtual Firewall Details
docs.description: As the virtual firewalls are created, Cloud Console users can view the enabled Virtual Firewalls in the Networking > Virtual Firewalls section. The following details are shown:

docs.title: Volume Management
docs.description: To view the disks attached to this Instance, navigate to the Networking, select a Virtual Firewall and access the Volumes tab.

docs.title: About Virtual Private Clouds
docs.description: Details about Virtual Private Cloud operations and actions can be found in their respective sections.

docs.title: Create, List and View VPCs
docs.description: Creating a VPC

docs.title: Creating VPC Subnets and Tiers
docs.description: VPCs follow the convention of 3-tiered architectures, with web, app, and DB tiers forming the norm. You can, however, configure these tiers to suit your application architecture or just follow the common convention.

docs.title: IPv4 Addresses and VPC
docs.description: IPv4 Addresses are an integral part of using VPC networking, and need to be used to access various components of the VPC. By default, a public IPv4 Address is assigned to the VR which can communicate through the internet to transmit traffic to/from the VR. This IPv4 can also be used for configuring remote access (L2TP) and site-to-site (IPSec) VPN connections.

next.title: Managing Access Control on VPC Subnets

docs.title: Managing Access Control on VPC Subnets
docs.description: Access control policies can be created using Access Control Lists (ACL) and configuring rules within these ACL (called ACL Rules). An ACL can then be applied to any tier within the VPC. These policies govern what traffic is allowed or restricted into and from your VPC network tiers.

docs.title: Managing VPC Instances
docs.description: Viewing VPC Instances

docs.title: Overview
docs.description: To view a list of sections and the various operations or actions you can perform by going inside the particular section, click on the VPC name. Below the VPC name is an informational view where you can find the details below.

docs.title: Reconfiguring a VPC
docs.description: The Reconfigure section/tab lists your current subscription details and allows you to reconfigure the networking pack or switch between hourly and monthly pricing.

previous.title: Managing Access Control on VPC Subnets

docs.title: VPC Management and Basic Operations
docs.description: VPC management offers the following operations. These are basic VPC management actions and don't have any impact on the actual network configurations.

next.title: Difference Between L2 Networks and VPC

docs.title: Difference Between L2 Networks and VPC
docs.description: L2 Networks

next.title: Working with VPN Connections in a VPC

docs.title: Working with VPN Connections in a VPC
docs.description: The VPC feature on Apiculus provides advanced networking capabilities for use with VPN gateways and connections over an IPSec tunnel. Apiculus gives the ability to create virtual private networks (VPN) to access virtual machines inside a VPC.

previous.title: Difference Between L2 Networks and VPC

docs.title: Wallet and Transactions
docs.description: The Wallet and Transactions section gives users a quick view of their account balance and transaction history. It shows available funds, including wallet and voucher balances, and offers basic tools to search, filter, and download transaction records. This section helps users keep track of their billing and payments in one place.

link.description: For all practical purposes, the service balance acts as your wallet balance or purchase capacity at any given point of time. The service balance is an aggregation of your credit limit, total payments made, and the total charges incurred on your account. Your service balance is always displayed on the top bar and gets updated in real-time when a transaction or a charge is recorded.
Account Centre on Yntraa cloud console is the non-technical (commercial and support)dashboard that can be used for viewing and managing all non-technical aspects of your Yntraa cloud account.

Yntraa offers Cloud Console users the ability to deploy highly available Kubernetes clusters for their cloud-native development, deployment and DevOps use cases. The Yntraa Kubernetes Service (YK8s) extends basic and advanced cluster management functionalities via the Cloud Console UI and the kubectl utility.

Before creating a Linux instance, it is important to plan the architecture, networking and access to the Linux Instances. Yntraa cloud console offers a highly usable and visual way of working with and operating Linux Instances using the Yntraa cloud console.
Instances are computing units that provide you with resources to run your applications/workloads. RHEL Instances are virtual machines that run the images of RHEL Operating System.
Instances are computing units that provide you with resources to run your applications/workloads. Windows Instances are virtual machines that run the images of Windows Operating Systems.
The autoscale feature allows a system to adjust its computing resources based on the changing workload automatically.

Instances are computing units that provide you with resources to run your applications/workloads. Linux Instances are virtual machines that run the images of Linux Operating Systems, for example, Ubuntu, CentOS, Rocky, Oracle, Debian, etc.
A Network Address Translation (NAT) gateway is a networking component in cloud and on-premises environments that enables resources within a private subnet (such as virtual machines or containers).
A Network Address Translation (NAT) gateway is a networking component in cloud and on-premises environments that enables resources within a private subnet (such as virtual machines or containers).

A Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) is a software-defined networking capability on Yntraa that allows cloud users to simulate a traditional private cloud environment on a public cloud. Using VPC, users can control a host of features and configurations including subnet/tier management, access control using ACL, NAT-ing using IPv4, managing site-to-site or remote VPN connections, or in some advanced cases, terminating their MPLS connections directly on the VPC. On Yntraa, VPC is delivered using a virtual router (VR).
A Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) is a software-defined networking capability on Yntraa that allows cloud users to simulate a traditional private cloud environment on a public cloud.
IPv4 Addresses form the backbone of networking operations on Yntraa.

Virtual Firewalls enables you to use Virtual Firewalls in a Virtualized Network Function (VNF) style, which include support for multiple VLANs and Public IPv4 addresses and automated service activation. This allows for more robust firewall management capabilities.
Yntraa supports the delivery and management of Virtual Firewall Instances (VFI) in an as-a-service model.

L2 networks, often called data link layer networks, play a crucial role in computer networking by establishing direct connections between devices in a local network and ensuring data packets are delivered accurately to their intended destination.
Yntraa servers can be used as authoritative name server(s) for managing DNS records.
The Yntraa Load Balancer Instances (YBI) Service allows Cloud Console users to create load balancer appliances, which are Linux Instances pre-loaded with a Citrix NetScaler VPX image.

Enable seamless connectivity between cloud infrastructure and resources, providing efficient data flow and communication. It manages virtual networks, firewalls, and security groups to ensure secure and scalable cloud operations.

metadata.title: MDX Blog Post
metadata.description: Blog posts support Docusaurus Markdown features, such as MDX.

blogPosts.content:
Blog posts support [Docusaurus Markdown features](https://docusaurus.io/docs/markdown-features), such as [MDX](https://mdxjs.com/).

:::tip

Use the power of React to create interactive blog posts.

:::

{/* truncate */}

For example, use JSX to create an interactive button:

```js
<button onClick={() => alert('button clicked!')}>Click me!</button>
```

<button onClick={() => alert('button clicked!')}>Click me!</button>

default.title: Markdown page example
default.description: You don't need React to write simple standalone pages.
